channel.title: Methodology on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Methodology on emsenn.net

item.title: discipline
item.description:
A discipline is a domain of knowledge with its own methods. What makes a discipline a discipline — as opposed to a topic or a loose collection of ideas — is methodological independence: it has its own standards for what counts as evidence, how claims are validated, and how inquiry proceeds.

Mathematics proves. Sociology observes and theorizes. Philosophy argues. Ecology measures and models. These are not interchangeable. The methods of a discipline are among its most important content — they determine what kind of knowledge the discipline can produce and what forms that knowledge takes.

item.title: school
item.description:
A school is a named theoretical tradition within a discipline. Schools share the discipline’s subject matter but differ in how they approach it: what questions they prioritize, what methods they use, what epistemological commitments they hold, and who they cite as foundational thinkers.

Critical pedagogy and constructivism are both schools within pedagogy. They both study teaching and learning. But critical pedagogy (Freire, hooks) foregrounds power and liberation, while constructivism (Piaget, Vygotsky) foregrounds the learner’s construction of knowledge. The difference is not just emphasis — it produces different methods, different questions, and different kinds of knowledge.

item.title: Structured-work paradigms: a cross-disciplinary survey
item.description:
This text surveys structured-work methodologies across three disciplines — business/management, government/military, and scientific/academic research — to identify patterns that inform the design of agent workflows in the agential semioverse repository.

The survey was conducted via web research in March 2026. Each domain was researched independently to avoid cross-contamination of framing. Citations are provided per finding.

Cross-cutting patterns

Five patterns recur across all three domains. These are the findings most relevant to the emsemioverse’s workflow design.

item.title: Dialectics
item.description:
Dialectics

Dialectics is a method of reasoning through contradiction. In its most familiar form — thesis, antithesis, synthesis — it proposes that understanding advances when a position (thesis) encounters its negation (antithesis) and both are resolved into a higher unity (synthesis) that preserves and transcends both. The method has a long history, from Plato’s Socratic dialogues through Hegel’s philosophy of spirit to Marx’s materialist inversion, and it remains one of the dominant intellectual methods in critical theory, political analysis, and journalism.

item.title: Archaeology
item.description: Archaeology is Foucault’s method for analyzing the conditions that make a body of knowledge possible in a given historical period, without treating those conditions as steps in a story of progress. Where a conventional history of ideas traces how one thinker influenced another — how Darwin built on Malthus, how Pasteur corrected Lister — archaeology asks a different question: what rules of formation had to be in place for these statements to appear at all?

item.title: Archaeology (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the overview and want to understand Foucault’s method for analyzing discourse.

Learning goal: explain how archaeology analyzes the conditions that make knowledge possible in a period, and apply the method to a concrete example.

The question archaeology asks

In the 18th Century, European naturalists classified living things by arranging them in tables according to visible features — the shape of leaves, the structure of flowers, the arrangement of teeth. In the 19th Century, biologists classified living things by tracing their descent from common ancestors through hidden developmental processes. Both were studying “nature.” But the rules governing what counted as a valid claim about nature had changed completely.

item.title: Biopower and Governmentality (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the power/knowledge lesson and want to understand how Foucault’s analysis applies to the governance of populations.

Learning goal: explain the shift from sovereign power to biopower, describe how governmentality organizes the management of populations, and analyze a contemporary institution in biopolitical terms.

From the scaffold to the census

In 1757, the French state executed Robert-François Damiens by public torture — his body was the site where sovereign power made itself visible. By the mid-19th Century, the same state was conducting national censuses, tracking birth and death rates, regulating sanitation, and managing public health campaigns. The state’s relationship to its subjects had changed: it was no longer primarily threatening them with death. It was managing their lives.

item.title: Foucault Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers new to critical theory who want to understand what Foucault’s work does and why it matters.

Learning goal: explain what questions Foucault asks, how his two methods (archaeology and genealogy) relate to each other, and why his analysis of power differs from common understandings.

A different kind of question

Most political theory asks: who has power, and is their use of it legitimate? Michel Foucault asked a different question: how does power work?

item.title: Genealogy
item.description:
Genealogy is a method for tracing how present-day concepts, institutions, and practices emerged through contingency, conflict, and power rather than through rational development or natural necessity. Where most histories explain the present as the outcome of progress or design, genealogy shows that the present is the product of struggles that could have gone otherwise.

Friedrich Nietzsche originated the method in On the Genealogy of Morals (1887). He asked where the concepts “good” and “evil” came from. His answer was that “good” originally meant “noble” — a term the powerful used to describe themselves — and “evil” was the invention of the powerless, who revalued strength as morally corrupt. Moral concepts did not descend from universal reason. They descended from specific historical confrontations between groups with competing interests.

item.title: Genealogy (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the archaeology lesson and want to understand Foucault’s second major historical method.

Learning goal: conduct a basic genealogical analysis — identify the descent and emergence of a concept or practice, and explain why genealogy refuses the search for origins.

Why this matters

Consider the modern job interview. Most people treat it as natural — of course employers evaluate candidates by asking them questions in a formal meeting. It feels like the obvious way to hire someone.

item.title: Power/Knowledge (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the genealogy lesson and want to understand the mechanism that genealogical analysis reveals.

Learning goal: explain how power and knowledge constitute each other, identify the examination as the paradigmatic mechanism of power/knowledge, and analyze a concrete institution in these terms.

The common assumption genealogy overturns

Most people hold two assumptions about the relationship between power and knowledge:

  1. Knowledge is neutral. Scientists discover facts; politicians may misuse those facts, but the facts themselves are innocent.
  2. Knowledge liberates. The more we know, the freer we are. Education, research, and transparency are weapons against oppression.

Michel Foucault’s concept of power/knowledge challenges both assumptions — not by saying that knowledge is bad, but by showing that the production of knowledge is never separable from the exercise of power.

item.title: Exploring the semantics of The Late Marx citers
item.description:
import csv, json, re, time, random
import requests

OUTFILE = "abstracts.csv"
CONTACT_EMAIL = "emsenn@emsenn.net"
MAX_RESULTS = 200
SLEEP = 1.2
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)

QUERY_ANDERSON = '"Kevin B. Anderson" "The Late Marx and the Russian Road"'
QUERY_CONTROL  = 'Marxism OR "Karl Marx"'
MARX_FILTER = re.compile(r"(marx|marxism|historical materialism|capitalis\w*|communis\w*|class struggle)", re.I)
BAD_SUBJECTS = {"chemistry", "physics", "materials", "engineering", "medicine", "biology", "geology"}

def query_crossref(query, rows=100, cursor="*"):
    url = "https://api.crossref.org/works"
    params = {"query.bibliographic": query, "rows": rows, "cursor": cursor, "mailto": CONTACT_EMAIL}
    while True:
        r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code != 200:
            print("Error", r.status_code, r.text)
            break
        data = r.json()
        items = data["message"]["items"]
        for it in items:
            yield it
        cursor_next = data["message"].get("next-cursor")
        if not cursor_next or len(items) == 0:
            break
        params["cursor"] = cursor_next
        time.sleep(SLEEP)

def clean(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", str(text)).strip()

def extract(it):
    doi = it.get("DOI", "")
    title = clean(" ".join(it.get("title", [])))
    abstract = clean(it.get("abstract", ""))
    year = None
    for fld in ("published-print","published-online","created"):
        if fld in it and "date-parts" in it[fld]:
            year = it[fld]["date-parts"][0][0]
            break
    authors = ", ".join([a.get("family","") for a in it.get("author",[]) if "family" in a])
    publisher = it.get("publisher", "")
    subjects = [s.lower() for s in it.get("subject", [])]
    lang = it.get("language","").lower() if it.get("language") else ""
    return {"doi":doi,"title":title,"abstract":abstract,"year":year,
            "authors":authors,"publisher":publisher,"subjects":subjects,"lang":lang}

def is_marxist(rec):
    """Heuristic: English + humanities/social + Marx lexicon"""
    if rec["lang"] and not rec["lang"].startswith("en"):
        return False
    if any(bad in rec["subjects"] for bad in BAD_SUBJECTS):
        return False
    text = f"{rec['title']} {rec['abstract']}".lower()
    return bool(MARX_FILTER.search(text))

# --- Main collection ---
def collect(query, label):
    records = []
    for item in query_crossref(query, rows=100):
        rec = extract(item)
        if not is_marxist(rec):
            continue
        rec["label"] = label
        records.append(rec)
        if len(records) >= MAX_RESULTS:
            break
    return records

print("Fetching Anderson-related records…")
anderson_records = collect(QUERY_ANDERSON, "cites_anderson")

print("Fetching Marxist control records…")
control_records = collect(QUERY_CONTROL, "control")

rows = anderson_records + control_records
random.shuffle(rows)

# --- Write CSV ---
with open(OUTFILE, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=[
        "label","year","title","authors","publisher","doi","abstract"
    ])
    writer.writeheader()
    for r in rows:
        writer.writerow({k:r.get(k,"") for k in writer.fieldnames})

print(f"Wrote {len(rows)} records to {OUTFILE}")
print("Sample titles:")
for r in rows[:10]:
    print(f"- [{r['label']}] {r['year']} {r['title'][:80]}…")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
explore-anderson-citations.py
---------------------------------
Quick analysis of whether Anderson-citing works use more 'moral' or 'method'
language, using a simple keyword frequency approach on abstracts.csv.

Input CSV must contain columns: label, text (abstract or concatenated text).
Label values: 'cites_anderson' or 'control'.

Outputs group means and 95% confidence intervals per 1000 words.
"""

import re, csv, statistics as stats
from collections import Counter

# --- Keyword patterns ---
METHOD = r"\b(method|methods|measurement|data(set)?|survey|fieldwork|interview|sampling|regress\w*|model\w*|estimate\w*|coding|reliab\w*|validi\w*|operationali\w*|variable\w*|hypothes\w*|test(ed|ing)?|significan\w*|simulat\w*|network\w*|experiment\w*|appendix|replicat\w*|code|archive|corpus|algorithm)\b"
MORAL  = r"\b(redempt\w*|inclusion|inclusive|recognit\w*|plural\w*|eurocentr\w*|decolonial\w*|coloniality|humanism|ethic(s|al)\b(?!\s*protocol)|empath\w*|conscience|guilt|atone\w*|moral\w*|legitim\w*|rehabilitat\w*|reconciliat\w*|solidarit\w*|utopian\w*|hopeful|hope|salvat\w*|justice|injustic\w*|violenc\w*|suffer\w*|oppress\w*|emancipat\w*|dignit\w*|liberat\w*|redress|repair|healing)\b"

METHOD_RE = re.compile(METHOD, re.I)
MORAL_RE  = re.compile(MORAL, re.I)

def normalize(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).lower()

def count_keywords(text, pattern):
    return len(pattern.findall(text))

def load_records(path="abstracts.csv"):
    records = []
    with open(path, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            label = row.get("label","").strip()
            # Concatenate title + abstract for analysis
            text = normalize((row.get("title","") or "") + " " + (row.get("abstract","") or ""))
            if not text:
                continue
            records.append({"label":label, "text":text})
    return records

def compute_scores(records):
    results = []
    for rec in records:
        words = rec["text"].split()
        n_words = len(words) if words else 1
        method_count = count_keywords(rec["text"], METHOD_RE)
        moral_count  = count_keywords(rec["text"], MORAL_RE)
        # Per 1000 words
        mth_per_1k = method_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        mor_per_1k = moral_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        results.append({
            "label": rec["label"],
            "method_per_1k": mth_per_1k,
            "moral_per_1k": mor_per_1k
        })
    return results

def group_stats(results):
    groups = {}
    for r in results:
        groups.setdefault(r["label"], {"method":[], "moral":[]})
        groups[r["label"]]["method"].append(r["method_per_1k"])
        groups[r["label"]]["moral"].append(r["moral_per_1k"])
    return groups

def summarize(vals):
    if not vals:
        return (0.0,0.0,0.0)
    mean = stats.mean(vals)
    if len(vals) > 1:
        stdev = stats.pstdev(vals)
        # 95% CI ~ mean ± 1.96 * (stdev / sqrt(n))
        se = stdev / (len(vals) ** 0.5)
        ci_low = mean - 1.96 * se
        ci_high = mean + 1.96 * se
    else:
        ci_low = ci_high = mean
    return (mean, ci_low, ci_high)

def main():
    records = load_records()
    results = compute_scores(records)
    groups = group_stats(results)

    print("Group\tType\tMean\tCI_low\tCI_high\tN")
    for label, vals in groups.items():
        for kind in ("method","moral"):
            arr = vals[kind]
            mean, lo, hi = summarize(arr)
            print(f"{label}\t{kind}\t{mean:.2f}\t{lo:.2f}\t{hi:.2f}\t{len(arr)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
